category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Similar Questions/Part B/Real Analysis — Continuity and Uniform Continuity

questiontext.text: A continuous one-to-one function \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) must be:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Strictly monotone

A continuous injective function on \(\mathbb{R}\) must be strictly monotone (either strictly increasing or strictly decreasing) by the Intermediate Value Theorem. If \(f\) were not monotone, it would take some value twice, contradicting injectivity. It need not be onto (e.g., \(f(x)=e^x\) is strictly increasing but not surjective onto \(\mathbb{R}\)).

name.text: Continuity - Uniform Continuity on Compact Set

questiontext.text: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous, then \(f\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Uniformly continuous on \([a,b]\)

Heine–Cantor theorem: a continuous function on a compact set is uniformly continuous. \([a,b]\) is compact (closed and bounded in \(\mathbb{R}\)). This does NOT hold on open intervals: \(f(x)=1/x\) is continuous but not uniformly continuous on \((0,1]\).

questiontext.text: If \(f:[0,1]\to[0,1]\) is continuous, then by the Intermediate Value Theorem:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(f\) has a fixed point (i.e., \(f(c)=c\) for some \(c\in[0,1]\))

Consider \(g(x)=f(x)-x\). Then \(g(0)=f(0)\geq0\) and \(g(1)=f(1)-1\leq0\). By IVT, \(g(c)=0\) for some \(c\in[0,1]\), i.e., \(f(c)=c\). This is Brouwer's fixed point theorem in 1D.

questiontext.text: The function \(f(x)=\dfrac{1}{x}\) is NOT uniformly continuous on \((0,1)\) because:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) For any \(\delta>0\), take \(x=\delta/2\) and \(y=\delta/3\): \(|x-y|<\delta\) but \(|f(x)-f(y)|\to\infty\)

More precisely: take \(x_n=1/n\), \(y_n=1/(n+1)\). Then \(|x_n-y_n|=1/(n(n+1))\to0\) but \(|f(x_n)-f(y_n)|=1\). So no single \(\delta\) works for all pairs, violating uniform continuity.

answer.text: \(f\) is not differentiable at some point
Points near 0 with small \(|x-y|\) can have large \(|f(x)-f(y)|\)

questiontext.text: If \(|f(x)-f(y)|\leq K|x-y|\) for all \(x,y\) (Lipschitz with constant \(K\)), then \(f\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Uniformly continuous

Given \(\epsilon>0\), choose \(\delta=\epsilon/K\). Then \(|x-y|<\delta\Rightarrow|f(x)-f(y)|\leq K|x-y|

questiontext.text: If \(f:A\to B\) and \(g:B\to C\) are both uniformly continuous, then \(g\circ f\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Uniformly continuous

Given \(\epsilon>0\), uniform continuity of \(g\) gives \(\delta_1>0\) such that \(|u-v|<\delta_1\Rightarrow|g(u)-g(v)|<\epsilon\). Uniform continuity of \(f\) gives \(\delta>0\) such that \(|x-y|<\delta\Rightarrow|f(x)-f(y)|<\delta_1\). Chain: \(|x-y|<\delta\Rightarrow|g(f(x))-g(f(y))|<\epsilon\).

answer.text: Continuous but not necessarily uniformly continuous

questiontext.text: \(f\) is continuous at \(a\) means: for every \(\epsilon>0\), there exists \(\delta>0\) such that:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(|x-a|<\delta\Rightarrow|f(x)-f(a)|<\epsilon\)

The \(\epsilon\)-\(\delta\) definition of continuity at a point. Uniform continuity requires a \(\delta\) that works for ALL points simultaneously (not just at one fixed \(a\)). Sequential continuity is equivalent: \(x_n\to a\Rightarrow f(x_n)\to f(a)\).

questiontext.text: The function \(f(x)=\sqrt{x}\) on \([0,1]\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Uniformly continuous but not Lipschitz at 0

\(\sqrt{x}\) is continuous on the compact set \([0,1]\), so uniformly continuous by Heine–Cantor. However, \(|f(x)-f(0)|/|x-0|=1/\sqrt{x}\to\infty\) as \(x\to0^+\), so it is NOT Lipschitz. It is Hölder continuous with exponent \(1/2\): \(|\sqrt{x}-\sqrt{y}|\leq|x-y|^{1/2}\).

answer.text: Uniformly continuous but not Lipschitz at \(0\)

questiontext.text: The Extreme Value Theorem states: if \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous, then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(f\) attains its maximum and minimum on \([a,b]\)

A continuous function on a compact set is bounded and attains its bounds. On \([a,b]\) (compact), \(f\) achieves \(\sup f\) and \(\inf f\). This fails on open intervals: \(f(x)=x\) on \((0,1)\) has sup 1 and inf 0 but does not attain them.

answer.text: \(f\) attains its maximum and minimum values on \([a,b]\)
\(f\) is uniformly continuous only if it is Lipschitz

questiontext.text: The function \(f(x)=\sin(1/x)\) for \(x\neq0\) and \(f(0)=0\) has at \(x=0\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) An essential (oscillatory) discontinuity

\(\sin(1/x)\) oscillates infinitely between \(-1\) and \(1\) as \(x\to0\). The limit \(\lim_{x\to0}\sin(1/x)\) does not exist. So the discontinuity is essential (neither removable nor a jump discontinuity). Contrast with \(x\sin(1/x)\to0\) which has a removable discontinuity at 0.

questiontext.text: A monotone function \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) can have at most:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Countably many discontinuities

Each discontinuity of a monotone function is a jump discontinuity. To each jump at \(x_0\), we can associate a distinct rational number in the jump interval \((f(x_0^-),f(x_0^+))\). Since the rational intervals are disjoint and \(\mathbb{Q}\) is countable, the discontinuities form a countable set.

questiontext.text: Weierstrass approximation theorem states: every continuous \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) can be uniformly approximated by:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Polynomials

Weierstrass (1885): for any continuous \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) and \(\epsilon>0\), there exists a polynomial \(p\) such that \(\sup_{x\in[a,b]}|f(x)-p(x)|<\epsilon\). The Stone–Weierstrass theorem generalizes this. Bernstein polynomials provide an explicit construction.

answer.text: Rational functions with integer coefficients only

questiontext.text: The Dirichlet function \(f(x)=\begin{cases}1&x\in\mathbb{Q}\\0&x\notin\mathbb{Q}\end{cases}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (D) Discontinuous at every point of \(\mathbb{R}\)

For any \(x\in\mathbb{R}\) and any \(\delta>0\), the interval \((x-\delta,x+\delta)\) contains both rationals and irrationals. So \(f\) takes values 0 and 1 in every neighbourhood of \(x\), hence \(\lim_{t\to x}f(t)\) does not exist. The function is discontinuous everywhere — and not Riemann integrable.

questiontext.text: Thomae's function \(f(x)=\begin{cases}1/q&x=p/q\in\mathbb{Q},\;\gcd(p,q)=1,\;q>0\\0&x\notin\mathbb{Q}\end{cases}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Continuous at every irrational, discontinuous at every rational

At irrationals: for any \(\epsilon>0\), the rationals \(p/q\) with \(f(p/q)=1/q\geq\epsilon\) have \(q\leq1/\epsilon\), so only finitely many; we can find \(\delta\) avoiding them. At rationals: every neighbourhood contains irrationals where \(f=0\neq f(p/q)\). Thomae's function is Riemann integrable (discontinuous only on a countable — hence measure zero — set).

answer.text: Continuous at every irrational, discontinuous at every rational
Continuous at every rational, discontinuous at every irrational

questiontext.text: \(f\) is NOT continuous at \(a\) if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) There exists a sequence \(x_n\to a\) but \(f(x_n)\not\to f(a)\)

Sequential criterion for discontinuity: \(f\) is discontinuous at \(a\) iff there is a sequence \(x_n\to a\) with \(f(x_n)\not\to f(a)\). This is the negation of the sequential characterization of continuity (\(x_n\to a\Rightarrow f(x_n)\to f(a)\)).

answer.text: There exists a sequence \(x_n\to a\) with \(f(x_n)\not\to f(a)\)

questiontext.text: If \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) satisfies \(f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y)\) for all \(x,y\in\mathbb{R}\) and \(f\) is continuous at one point, then:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(f(x)=cx\) for some constant \(c=f(1)\)

Cauchy's functional equation: additive + continuous (at one point) implies \(f(x)=cx\). Without continuity, there exist pathological solutions (using Hamel basis of \(\mathbb{R}\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\)). Continuity at one point extends to continuity everywhere for additive functions.

questiontext.text: If \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous and \(A\subset\mathbb{R}\) is connected, then \(f(A)\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Connected (hence an interval)

Continuous functions preserve connectedness. In \(\mathbb{R}\), connected sets are exactly intervals. So the continuous image of an interval is an interval — this is the IVT generalized. Note: continuous images of compact sets are compact; continuous images of connected sets are connected.

questiontext.text: Which of the following is uniformly continuous on \((0,1)\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(f(x)=\sin x\)

\(\sin x\) is Lipschitz on all of \(\mathbb{R}\) (with constant 1), hence uniformly continuous on \((0,1)\). \(1/x\) is not uniformly continuous (unbounded near 0). \(x^{-1/2}\) similarly diverges near 0. \(\ln x\) also diverges near 0 and is not uniformly continuous on \((0,1)\).

name.text: Continuity - Continuous Function Vanishing at Infinity

questiontext.text: Let \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) be continuous with \(\lim_{|x|\to\infty}f(x)=0\). Then \(f\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Bounded and uniformly continuous on \(\mathbb{R}\)

Since \(f\to0\) at \(\pm\infty\), for any \(\epsilon>0\) there exists \(R\) with \(|f(x)|<\epsilon\) for \(|x|>R\). On \([-R,R]\) (compact), \(f\) is bounded and uniformly continuous. Combining: \(f\) is bounded on all of \(\mathbb{R}\) and also uniformly continuous (the parts near infinity are controlled by smallness of \(f\)).

questiontext.text: Let \(f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}\) be continuous and one-to-one. Which property is necessarily true?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(f\) is strictly monotone

A continuous injective map on \(\mathbb{R}\) must be strictly monotone (IVT argument). It need not be onto (\(e^x\) is strictly increasing but range is \((0,\infty)\neq\mathbb{R}\)), need not be bounded (\(f(x)=x\) is unbounded), and need not be differentiable (there exist strictly monotone continuous non-differentiable functions).
